source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 4.3 Display and Identification (pp. 38-40)

questions.why: clear shifts the display so the prompt sits at the top; Ctrl+L has the same effect.

questions.q: Which key sequence has the same effect as the 'clear' command?
questions.why: Ctrl+L clears the screen just like clear; Ctrl+C interrupts the running command instead.

questions.q: Print the text 123 to the screen WITHOUT a trailing newline.
questions.why: echo prints a string and adds a newline by default; the -n option suppresses that trailing newline.

questions.q: Which echo option interprets backslash sequences such as \n (newline) and \t (tab)?
questions.why: -e enables interpretation of escape sequences (\n line break, \t horizontal tab, \a beep, ...); -n only removes the trailing newline.

questions.q: Display the current date and time.
questions.why: date with no arguments prints the current date and time (its format depends on the $LANG environment variable).

questions.q: Display today's date in YYYY-MM-DD format using a date format string.
questions.why: +%F is the shorthand format for a YYYY-MM-DD date; format specifiers are introduced with a leading '+'.

questions.q: Which date option is used to CHANGE the system date and time?
questions.why: date -s "yyyy-MM-dd hh:mm[:ss]" sets the clock; -d merely displays a given date without changing the system.

questions.q: Show the UID, GID and groups of the user 'rockstar'.
questions.why: id shows user and group information; with no argument it reports the current user, with a name it reports that user.

questions.q: Print just the login name of the current user.
questions.why: whoami displays the login of the current user; 'who' instead lists all logged-in users and their terminals.

questions.q: List the users currently logged in, with their terminals and login times.
questions.why: who lists logged-in users (tty = physical terminal, pts = virtual console); its -r option also shows the runlevel.
